title: The Golden Globes Nominations: Surprises and Snubs | The Oscars Show (Ep. 104)
format: Sean and Amanda dig through the morning's Golden Globe nominations, from Vice's category-fraud haul to Roma's bizarre exclusion from Best Picture, then run down the season's stock ups, stock downs, surprises, and snubs.

films.title: Vice
films.note: Led the field with six nominations, including Best Picture in comedy, a placement Amanda calls blatant category fraud. Sean thinks the international, explain-America appeal of Adam McKay's Dick Cheney satire helped it land.

films.title: Green Book
films.note: One of five nominations Sean and Amanda group with BlacKkKlansman as movies that read to an international voting body as explaining an American racial tension.

films.title: BlacKkKlansman
films.note: Grouped with Green Book as a Globes-friendly explain-America pick.

films.title: Bohemian Rhapsody
films.note: Competed in drama alongside A Star Is Born, which Sean and Amanda find odd given how loosely the Globes assign the comedy/musical label.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: The drama frontrunner, and the movie Sean says makes the most sense for a straightforward Best Picture run.

films.title: Crazy Rich Asians
films.note: Amanda is glad to see it and Constance Wu in the conversation, though she's not sure it carries into the Oscars.

films.title: The Favourite
films.note: Nominated as a comedy, which both hosts find funny given how grim much of the film actually is.

films.title: Roma
films.note: Alfonso Cuarón gets director and screenplay nods, but the film itself is barred from Best Picture drama or comedy because the Globes' bylaws say a foreign-language nominee can only compete in the foreign film category.

films.title: Vice
films.note: Sam Rockwell's supporting nod for playing George W. Bush comes at the expense of Sam Elliott.

films.title: Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri
films.note: Rockwell's Oscar and Globe win last year for this film, which Sean says he's essentially repeating in the same slot this year.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: Sam Elliott's nomination comes for a body-of-work role Sean compares to Gary Oldman's position last year.

films.title: BlacKkKlansman
films.note: No screenplay nomination despite expectations, but John David Washington and Adam Driver both land acting nods.

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: Shut out of every acting category, which Sean says makes its Best Picture nomination feel more like a formality than real enthusiasm.

films.title: Mary Poppins Returns
films.note: Amanda expects a bigger campaign push built around Emily Blunt and Lin-Manuel Miranda.

films.title: First Man
films.note: Nominated only for Justin Hurwitz's score, missing Gosling, Chazelle, and Best Picture, which Sean blames on a bad October release date.

films.title: First Reformed
films.note: Ethan Hawke's total shutout is the snub that bothers Amanda most; she calls him a movie star being ignored by a body that usually loves a good press tour.

films.title: Crazy Rich Asians
films.note: Constance Wu's nomination, which Amanda's happy about.

films.title: Eighth Grade
films.note: Elsie Fisher's nomination for a performance Sean calls a star turn, backed by an unusually strong press-tour run for a teenager.

films.title: Tully
films.note: Charlize Theron's nod reads to Amanda as a put-a-famous-person-on-TV pick, even though she thinks the performance itself is very good.

films.title: Destroyer
films.note: Nicole Kidman's transformation role, which neither host finds surprising.

films.title: The Old Man & the Gun
films.note: Sean is thrilled Robert Redford got in and can't figure out why David Lowery's film never got a real theatrical push.

films.title: The Wife
films.note: Glenn Close's nomination, running joke being that neither host has actually seen the movie yet.

films.title: If Beale Street Could Talk
films.note: Barry Jenkins missing a Best Director nomination stings both hosts, and they flag Brian Tyree Henry's total shutout as an outright snub.
